figures.caption: Figure 1: Representative communication media in MAS. The figure compares natural-language messages, embedding-based representations, and direct KV-cache transfer. As the medium becomes less lossy, the receiver gains more direct access to the sender’s internal reasoning state.

Figure 3: Comparison of KV cache eviction granularities: 1) Global (shared across all layers and heads), 2) Layer-wise (shared across heads within each layer), and 3) Head-wise (independent for each KV head and each layer).

Table 1: Qwen3-14B accuracy (%) across datasets. Higher value is better. The L/ρ row reports total prompt length across rounds and compression ratio, lower value is better. L/L-OBF/H/H-OBF use a relay budget of k = 32 prompt tokens per round; all methods use p = 40 latent steps per round. Numbers are averaged over 3 independent runs. Best performances in each run are in bold.

Figure 4: Geometric illustration of Orthogonal BackFill (OBF). Rather than a literal highdimensional mapping, this 2D metaphor demonstrates the variable relationships: (a) The initial state defining the retained (Vkeep) and deleted (Vdel) value states. (b) The isolation of the orthogonal residual R, which captures information strictly outside the retained span Q (Eq. 4). (c) The derivation of the final injection vector δ. The residual is compressed via its principal subspace to form ∆ (Eq. 6), scaled by the demand ratio (Eq. 7), and uniformly fused back into the retained state. (Eq. 8)

Figure 2: KV-cache role decomposition in single-agent and multi-agent settings. Our multiagent decomposition follows the same functional view as standard single-agent KV compression, with aligned sink, candidate-like, and local-recent roles. The main difference is that multi-agent relay introduces inherited message history. The inset contrasts rolling-budget cache updates with our one-shot prompt-state selection.
